coredata.dc:title: Liquid chromatography methods as a solution to inaccuracies associated with purity assessment of citric acid-based carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wysocka, Alicja
Waluda, Łukasz
Konefał, Rafał
Kasprzyk, Wiktor
coredata.dc:description: Herein, several analytical techniques aimed on validation of separation effectiveness of free molecular fluorophores from citric acid-based carbon dots were elaborated and compared. Generally, chromatographic methods were found more reliable than steady-state optical measurements, pointing at the LC-FLD (liquid chromatography coupled to fluorescence detector) protocols as the most sensitive. Furthermore, the applicability of the frequently reported 1H NMR technique for confirmation of the absence of highly fluorescent free low-molecular weight species in carbon dots samples was called into question. It was found that, in the case of effectively PL emitting compounds (such as 5-oxo-2,3- dihydro-5H-[1,3]thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA)), the lowest concentration that could be detected with above technique (0.08 mg mL−1 of TPA) is higher than the concentration at which the particular fluorophore shows the brightest PL emission (∼0.01 mg mL−1 of TPA).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Citric acid
Purity assessment
Fluorescence
Mass spectrometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pure fluorophores and simulated CDs samples
Synthesis of CA-based carbon dots (CA-based CDs)
Preparation and purification of real CDs samples: cys-CDs, ur-CDs-blue, ur-CDs-green
Preparation of working solutions of the fluorophores and CA-based CDs
Analyses of real CDs samples at different purification stages

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of calibration curves obtained using HPLC methods of quantification of particular fluorophores in the presence and absence of CA-based CDs (green and blue dotted lines, respectively). Three different methods of signal detection are presented; Figures A, D, G refer to detection using a DAD-type detector; Figures B, E, H − FLD detector; Figures C, F, I detection on a mass spectrometer in positive ionization mode. Insight, in the upper part, shows chromatograms for a series of concentrations of studied fluorophore without CA-based CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

1H NMR spectra of TPA, HPPT fluorophores, neat CA-based CDs, mixture 1 (M1) of CA-based CDs with 7 μL (0.77 mg mL−1) TPA+8 μL (0.64 mg mL−1) HPPT+15 μL (0.16 mg mL−1) CZA, mixture 2 (M2) of CA-based CDs with 70 μL (0.77 mg mL−1) TPA+80 μL (0.64 mg mL−1) HPPT+15 μL (0.16 mg mL−1) CZA and M2 sample with 1 μL of DMF at 295 K.

1H NMR spectra of real CDs sample (ur-CDs-green) collected at various purification stages. Note that the spectrum magnification changes over different samples in order to enable appropriate recognition of the fluorophore (HPPT) signal. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison of linearity ranges for detection of TPA in the presence of CA-based CDs (simulated CDs samples), obtained via different techniques. Insight, on the bottom, represents photographs of CA-based CDs solutions spiked with different concentrations of TPA and excited at 350 nm. The red triangular labels refer to the concentrations of TPA in cys-CDs samples used in LC-FLD and NMR measurements (for actual concentrations determined at different stages of purification please see Table 2), while the red dotted line and the asterisks indicate TPA concentration exhibiting the strongest PL emission. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Linearity ranges obtained for detection of the fluorophores in studied solutions. Concentrations of the fluorophores are given in mg mL−1.

Fluorophore concentrations (mg mL−1) in real CDs samples determined by chromatographic methods at different dialysis times (TPA in cys-CDs, CZA in ur-CDs-blue and HPPT ur-CDs-green). Note that these values refer to the actual concentration of the fluorophores in the solutions obtained at different stages of sample purification therefore they do not match the linearity ranges presented in Fig. 4.

head.ce:title: Liquid chromatography methods as a solution to inaccuracies associated with purity assessment of citric acid-based carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology and Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24, 31-155 Kraków, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology and Physical Chemistry
Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry CAS, Heyrovského nám. 2, 162 06 Prague, Czech Republic
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry CAS, Heyrovského nám. 2, 162 06 Prague, Czech Republic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, several analytical techniques aimed on validation of separation effectiveness of free molecular fluorophores from citric acid-based carbon dots were elaborated and compared. Generally, chromatographic methods were found more reliable than steady-state optical measurements, pointing at the LC-FLD (liquid chromatography coupled to fluorescence detector) protocols as the most sensitive. Furthermore, the applicability of the frequently reported 1H NMR technique for confirmation of the absence of highly fluorescent free low-molecular weight species in carbon dots samples was called into question. It was found that, in the case of effectively PL emitting compounds (such as 5-oxo-2,3- dihydro-5H-[1,3]thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA)), the lowest concentration that could be detected with above technique (0.08 mg mL−1 of TPA) is higher than the concentration at which the particular fluorophore shows the brightest PL emission (∼0.01 mg mL−1 of TPA).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery, carbon dots (CDs), have attracted considerable attention due to their intriguing photophysical properties [1]. Accordingly, a number of novel possible ways of their utilization in many fascinating areas of research and life has grown exponentially e.g. in bioimaging, theranostics, energy conversion, and photocatalyzed hydrogen evolution [2–6]. Yet, some fundamental aspects concerning their structure-properties relationships have to be clarified to gain access to real-world applications. First step in achieving above goal is to obtain high-quality, representative nanomaterial – material that is free from impurities obscuring the true nature and hindering the rational design of the nano-objects under investigation [7]. Citric acid (CA), due to its high availability and ease of transformation, has become one of the most frequently used precursors for the bottom-up synthesis of CDs. A common approach of increasing photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield and red-shifting of optical characteristics of CA-based CDs is N-doping with specific amine precursors. The serious drawback of the latter process is that it produces substantial amounts of highly fluorescent low molecular fluorophores (derivatives of 2-pyridone), which dominate the photophysical features of post-synthetic mixtures [8–14]. In these lines, elaboration of efficient protocols for purification of bottom-up fabricated CDs from free citric acid-based molecular fluorophores (CBMFs), low-molecular byproducts and unreacted precursors has become a crucial step in reliable characterization of these nanomaterials [13,15–19]. Among many techniques used for this purpose, ultrafiltration, chromatography, and dialysis are the most trusted and the most frequently considered solutions [8,20–23]. However, the abovementioned methods have to be used with caution and by an experienced personnel to avoid errors associated with the incomplete separation of impurities from CDs (a comprehensive overview of the problem of inappropriate purification procedures in CDs was well described by Essner et al. [7] and Otten et al. [8]). In order to be able to verify whether the material under studies is free from low-molecular weight fluorescent impurities, elucidation of sensitive and selective validation techniques is necessary. Such a procedure should be rapid, relatively inexpensive, and simple to perform, as well as available in general purpose. Especially since there is a lack of commonly adopted guidelines and applied methodologies in this area. In the literature, methods focused mainly on steady-state UV–Vis absorption and PL emission measurements of CDs solutions, sometimes preceded by high-pressure chromatographic separation were reported [8,20–23]. In addition, Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) was employed to determine the presence of free low-molecular weight species in CDs solutions, more recently [24,25]. Although all the abovementioned techniques were proved to be highly precise in many specific areas of research and analytics, the scenario describing their failure as validation methods in the separation of CDs from free molecular fluorophores is easily imaginable. The latter may be true due to the fact that the vast of the reports describing above validation methods do not discuss the aspect of sensitivity and selectivity of the method in use. The significance of the above idea becomes evident considering that even small quantities of emissive species present in the sample can significantly affect the optical properties of the material under study – for fluorophores exhibiting high PL quantum yield, their concentration (sufficient for strong PL emission) might be far below the detection limit of the particular analytical technique.

Herein, a comparison of validation methods based on steady-state spectroscopic measurements, 1H NMR and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analyses, that are commonly used for confirmation of the absence of free molecular fluorophores in CDs, was introduced. The chromatographic studies were conducted using variety of detection techniques i.e. fluorescence detector (FLD), UV–vis absorbance diode array detector (DAD), and electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS). All studied methods were tested using standard analytical and sensitivity parameters typical for particular technique. By comparison of the linear ranges of quantification of the elaborated methods it was found that chromatographic methods are characterized by the highest sensitivity and selectivity, especially LC-FLD method should be pointed out, in this case, as the most suitable for the particular purpose.

In order to properly compare the analytical parameters of the methods under study, it was necessary to test them with solutions containing a mixture of the same analytes. Ideally if the analytes are well-known and relevant for the end-point users and the final application environment. Having above in mind, CDs synthesized through the bottom-up method using CA were chosen since CDs obtained from this precursor are amongst the most frequently reported in the literature [26,27]. Moreover, the structure and properties of low-molecular weight fluorophores forming in situ during the synthesis of CA-based CDs are thoroughly understood and well-documented hence they provide an ideal example to demonstrate the validation procedure (for testing the effectiveness of CDs purification from free low-molecular weight fluorophores) [28–30]. Thus, in this investigation CDs derived fluorophores (citrazinic acid (CZA), 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine-1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione (HPPT), 5-oxo-2,3-dihydro-5H-[1,3]thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA)) and CA-based CDs were used (please see experimental section for more details) [8,14,31–36]. Furthermore, common analytical parameters for each type of studied method were applied to provide possibly the most reliable starting point for comparisons. It’s worth pointing that by improving the analytical parameters of the equipment in use or using a special analytical setup (e.g. prolonged acquisition time, high sensitivity detectors, MRM in LCMS, cryoprobe in NMR, etc.), reaching much lower concentrations of the analytes is highly plausible, however, that kind of experimental setup is not commonly considered in CDs literature, probably due to lower accessibility. For the sake of clarity, the studies were conducted on three sets of samples i.e. solutions of pure fluorophores (i), simulated CDs samples (solution of CA-based CDs spiked with molecular fluorophores) (ii) and real CDs samples (solutions of CDs obtained after thermal treatment of CA and L-cysteine or urea, vide infra) (iii).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pure fluorophores and simulated CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, two types of representative series of solutions were prepared, one containing pure fluorophores and the other simulating the post-reaction scenario, i.e. a solution of CA-based CDs spiked with different concentrations of the fluorophores (Table S1). Then, UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra were collected for all samples. Afterwards, the samples were analyzed using LC-DAD-FLD-MS (HPLC coupled with three different detectors i.e. diode array for UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence detector and mass detector). Finally, appropriate calibration curves were prepared using the results obtained from each tested technique (Tables S2 and S3). The latter allowed reliable characterization of linear ranges for certain types of methods (Table 1 ). Afterwards, the 1H NMR protocol for fluorophore detection and quantification using the internal standard method was elaborated and tested.

Thus, steady-state measurements of absorption spectra of pure fluorophores revealed that it is possible to distinguish specific long wavelength bands of the analytes (>320 nm) from the baseline at concentrations as low as 1.46x10−4 mg mL−1 (TPA and HPPT) (Figures S1-S3). Further increase in fluorophores detection sensitivity was observed for fluorescence spectra measurements (Figures S4-S6). In this case, some analytes could be detected in a few ng mL−1 concentration range (TPA). On the other hand, for solutions containing CA-based CDs, the detection limits were about one order of magnitude higher for both techniques (Figures S4-S6). The latter is probably an effect of overlapping long wavelength absorption bands of studied fluorophores and an absorption tail typical for CDs (Figure S7). This overlap may lead to reabsorption and energy transfer events as well as to the so-called inner filter effect which in turn causes a decrease in the intensity of detected signals and overall sensitivity loss of the analytical method. At a glance, the detection limits of the methods discussed above are ample for confirming that the fluorescence of nanoparticles under study does not come from free molecular species (Table 1). However, having in mind that in real cases the situation is more complex, the results obtained from steady-state measurements may not be sufficient to lead to this conclusion. Accordingly, herein high-molecular weight fraction of CA-based CDs in all experiments was used, which show only negligible fluorescence (it is common that fractions of CDs with high molecular weight are less fluorescent) [7]. For smaller CDs which emit more efficiently, reabsorption, energy transfer, and inner filter effects would be more pronounced, while additional analytical problems would be caused by overlapping PL emission bands of CDs and fluorophores. Finally, it is easy to imagine that in real cases more than one fluorescent free low-molecular species contributes to the overall optical properties of the post-synthesis mixture, which makes simple steady-state spectroscopic measurements less applicable as a validation method in procedures aimed at the separation of CDs from free molecular fluorophores.

There are few common scenarios which could be considered for the analysis of complex mixtures such as CDs. They are based on the physical separation of specific constituents followed by spectroscopic detection (e.g. chromatography and electrophoresis methods) or visualization and quantification of characteristic structural features of individual components of the formulation (e.g. NMR, FTIR, and XPS). Among these techniques, chromatographic methods of CDs purity validation were elaborated and tested in the first instance. It was found that these methods provide the same (or better) sensitivity as steady-state measurements, and more importantly, they are not affected by the drawbacks associated with the latter techniques. Moreover, analyzing samples with these methods provides some additional structural information about particular constituents of the reaction mixture. For instance, the retention time of a specific component obtained from all detectors (DAD, FLD, and MS) is strictly associated with its polarity and molecular mass, while using a mass detector enables the elucidation of an exact mass of low-molecular fractions. Furthermore, specifically for the validation of the efficiency in purification of carbon dots from free low molecular weight fluorophores, it is highly important to know which fraction of a complex mixture exhibits PL emission, therefore using a fluorescence detector in this particular sense seems reasonable. Accordingly, it was found that analyses using a mass detector allow the detection of the lowest concentrations in the range of 10−4 − 10−5 mg mL−1 for each fluorophore (Fig. 1 and Figures S8-S10). The results from the fluorescence detector vary markedly depending on the particular fluorophore. In the case of TPA samples, it allowed to obtain a signal even at very low sample concentrations ranging from 10−6-10−7 mg mL−1. The other fluorophores examined, owing to their lower PL quantum yields, are not detectable at such low concentrations. Yet, the method still provides linear results for concentrations on the order of 10−4 mg mL−1. LC-DAD analysis allows the detection of higher concentrations than by other detectors. Detection of low concentrations of fluorophores is however limited; the analyses yielded results of at most 10−4 mg mL−1 from this detector (Table 1). For all chromatographic methods LOD and LOQ values were calculated (please see Table S4 and S5, respectively). For real-life samples of CDs in which several unknown free fluorophores are expected in the post-synthetic mixture, the use of the LC-FLD validation method seems to be the most reasonable. That is due to the fact that in this case, we would need to know only roughly PL excitation and emission characteristics of the target unbound PL emitting compounds to reliably validate their presence in particular fractions after specific separation process (e.g. dialysis, electrophoresis, chromatography). Abovementioned optical characteristics may be easily extracted from typical scheme representing excitation-dependent PL emission features which is commonly reported for CDs. Interestingly, in some cases, different slopes of calibration curves were noted for the solutions containing pure fluorophores than for mixtures of the fluorophores and CA-based CDs (particularly for the mass detector) (Fig. 1). Thus, it seems that the presence of CA-based CDs in the samples may affect the results − quantitative analysis of the free-floating fluorophores in real samples in reference to pure compound solutions may be inaccurate. However, this phenomenon requires more in-depth analysis and cannot be treated as a rule. This fact may be considered as a scratch on the overall satisfying sensitivity and selectivity of the HPLC methods under study in terms of application as a validation method in the process of separation of CDs from free molecular fluorophores.

In the further part of the investigation, sensitivity of the 1H NMR-based method reported in the literature for validation of the efficiency in the purification of CDs from free low molecular weight species was assessed [24,25]. Thus, Fig. 2 shows 1H NMR spectra of TPA, HPPT fluorophores, neat CA-based CDs (PI), mixture 1 (M1) of CA-based CDs (600 μL, 16.6 mg mL−1) with 7 μL (0.77 mg mL−1) TPA+8 μL (0.64 mg mL−1) HPPT+15 μL (0.16 mg mL−1) CZA, mixture 2 (M2) of CA-based CDs with 70 μL (0.77 mg mL−1) TPA+80 μL (0.64 mg mL−1) HPPT+15 μL (0.16 mg mL−1) CZA and M2 sample with 1 μL of DMF (Table S6). Spectra of TPA and HPPT are presented with the structures and assignment of resonances directly in the figure. A saturated aqueous solution of CZA was also measured, unfortunately, due to its low concentration (0.16 mg mL−1) and considerable number of possible tautomers, no signals were observed. In the case of CA-based CDs aqueous solution (D2O solution), mainly two broad signals are observed: first at δ = 9.50–5.50 ppm related to the aromatic protons and second at δ = 4.30–0.00 ppm from aliphatic as well as proton groups from close neighboring of heteroatom (N, O, etc.). The broad shape of the signals is caused by the low mobility of the protons of measured CA-based CDs and slow tumbling motions of entire nanoparticle in such an extent that they escape detection in high-resolution NMR spectra. This effect can be explained either by the low solubility of the sample, strong interactions between components (resulting in aggregates formations), or the presence of nanoparticles. The M1 and M2 solutions were prepared to assess the minimum concentration of fluorophores the 1H NMR spectroscopy is able to detect. In the spectrum of the mixture M1, no clearly new signals were observed, which means that this addition of TPA and HPPT was too low and the final concentrations of the fluorophores are below the detection limit of the spectrometer. In order to obtain a suitable spectrum, M2 solution was prepared. In the M2 spectrum required signals coming from molecular fluorophores were observed (highlighted in the figure). To prove that the method is valid for that kind of characterization, a known amount of internal standard was added (1 μL of DMF). Accordingly, calculation of the concentrations of specific fluorophores in the mixture became feasible using comparisons of specific integrals in the M2 + DMF sample. As a result, the concentration of analytes was calculated to be 0.08 mg mL−1 TPA and 0.064 mg mL−1 HPPT, which is close to the real concentration of samples prepared for the analysis (0.07 mg mL−1 TPA and 0.069 mg mL−1 HPPT). The differences between the values calculated from the NMR spectrum and the real concentrations may be associated with difficulties in the precise calculation of particular peak areas because of overlapping signals of CA-based CDs and heteroaromatic protons of the fluorophores. Nevertheless, the results obtained from NMR analyses clearly indicate that this technique fails to detect low concentrations of free low molecular weight fluorophores (<0.05 mg mL−1) (Fig. 2). The situation becomes even more serious when we realize that efficient fluorophores such as TPA exhibit the highest PL emission intensity at a concentration of ∼ 0.01 mg mL−1, which is below the detection limit of standard 1H NMR analysis as was shown here (the concentration of TPA at which the most efficient PL emission is observed was labeled with an asterisk on Fig. 4 for clarity) [37]. It is without a doubt, that the NMR method proposed in ref [24] and [25] was found very successful not only in the detection and elucidation of the chemical structure of molecular species in CDs but also shed light on the origin of chiral properties in some CDs and identified the importance of appropriate choice of CDs synthesis conditions to be able to achieve efficient carbonization of precursors. Nevertheless, the present studies showed that NMR might not be suitable as a validation method for the purification of CDs from strongly PL-emitting low molecular species due to sensitivity mismatch. Further drawbacks of using NMR as CDs purity validation method lay in the fact that it gives no information about the optical properties of specific components of the studied mixture, and requires samples to be dissolved in deuterated solvents, while vast of CDs purification protocols use aqueous media as the final solvent, making direct NMR analysis difficult. Another interesting implication of the obtained results is associated with the fact that some literature reports concerning the synthesis of CDs and polymer dots explain mechanisms of PL emission from obtained nanomaterials based on the absence of aromatic protons signals in the NMR spectrum [38]. It might happen that these reports have to be further reviewed in terms of the possible contribution of the free low-molecular weight fluorophores to the optical properties of investigated nanosystems. Nevertheless, it has to be stated that among all the techniques studied herein, NMR gives the most reliable and precise information about the chemical structure of the compounds under study. Yet, the latter is true only for concentrations of the analytes > 3x10−4 mol L−1.

The results obtained for the simplified CDs/fluorophore systems described above may be considered as a good starting point for further investigations of the applicability of studied analytical methods in real CDs samples. Considering the higher complexity of real CDs samples, three, well-known CDs systems based on the thermal treatment of CA in the presence of L-cysteine or urea were prepared (cys-CDs, ur-CDs-blue, ur-CDs-green) [8,14,31–36]. The cys-CDs were obtained through hydrothermal treatment of CA with L-cysteine, while ur-CDs-blue and ur-CDs-green were obtained after heating of aqueous solution of CA and urea in closed and open reactor, respectively (please see experimental section for details). The molecular fluorophores, recognized earlier, that form during these reactions include TPA, citrazinic acid, and HPPT. Firstly, the presence of the target fluorophores in the real CDs samples (raw reaction mixtures without purification) was verified using 1H NMR (see experimental section for the procedure of sample preparation for NMR). In this case, specific signals of free low molecular fluorophores in 1H NMR spectra of studied CDs were observed only for ur-CDs-green (HPPT fluorophore, δ = 6.0) (Fig. 3 ) and cys-CDs samples (TPA fluorophore, δ = 6.6, 6.5, 5.4, 3.9, 3.6) (Figure S11). For 1H NMR spectrum of ur-CDs-blue sample, for which citrazinic acid should be found in the reaction mixture, no specific signals coming from this compound could be observed (Figure S12) (similar as for the analyses on the simulated samples). Thus, using 1H NMR analyses it was possible to confirm the presence of specific molecular fluorophores in 2 out of 3 real CDs samples (without purification). In further experiments, 1H NMR was employed for the detection of the molecular fluorophores in CDs samples dialyzed for 24 h and/or 72 h against distilled water (dialysis membrane with 10 kDa cut-off was used), since dialysis is the most frequent method used for purification of CDs from molecular species. As expected from the experiment conducted for raw ur-CDs-blue sample, no citrazinic acid signals were detected (Figure S12 and Table S7). On the contrary, specific signals of TPA were present in 1H NMR spectrum of cys-CDs dialyzed for 24 h (and barely visible for sample dialyzed for 72 h) (Figure S11 and Table S7). For ur-CDs-green sample a characteristic signal of HPPT could be observed after 24 h of dialysis while it disappeared completely after 72 h of the purification process (Fig. 3 and Table S7). In summary, 1H NMR results indicated that the ur-CDs-blue samples are free from citrazinic acid, cys-CDs sample dialyzed for 72 h contains only scarce amount of TPA, while ur-CDs-green sample dialyzed for the same time is free from HPPT as fluorescent contaminant.

In summary, chromatographic methods allow for the detection of lower concentrations of target fluorophores in real CDs samples than it is possible with NMR, the latter, may help to avoid false interpretations of PL properties of particular CDs. A good representation of the results and comparison of particular techniques may be found in Fig. 4 which summarizes data obtained for determination of TPA concentration in the simulated and real CD samples.

In summary, several analytical techniques typically used for validation of the purification effectiveness of carbon dots from free low-molecular weight fluorophores were revisited here. Their sensitivity for detection of free molecular fluorophores in three sets of samples was tested i.e. pure fluorophores, CA-based carbon dots spiked with pure fluorophores, as well as frequently reported N-doped carbon dots prepared via thermal treatment of CA/urea, and CA/L-cysteine mixtures. It was found that for this specific purpose, the applicability of methods based on steady-state optical measurements is limited, due to the problems associated with possible overlapping of absorption and PL emission bands of diverse fluorescent species. Then, 1H NMR was found not appropriate as a CDs purity validation method since, among other factors, it’s not sufficiently sensitive to detect the low concentration of the studied fluorophores (concentration at which they show the brightest PL emission). Finally, HPLC-based techniques allow to overcome problems associated with both, overlapping signals and sensitivity mismatch of the abovementioned methods. By using LC-MS and LC-FLD, it is possible to detect specific molecular fluorophores (highly important for the carbon dot field) at levels not achievable for NMR. Careful use of these techniques may help to avoid false interpretations of PL properties of particular CDs and may push forward their rational design and development.
All chemicals were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CA-based carbon dots (CA-based CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CA-based CDs were prepared according to protocol described earlier with slight modifications [21]. Specifically, 1 g of anhydrous CA (Alfa Aesar, USA) was placed in a glass vial (15 mL) and heated using an aluminum heating block (230 °C, 120 min.). During the reaction a mixture changed a color from white to dark brown. Then, the mixture was dissolved in 5 mL of 1 mol L−1 NaOH using an ultrasonic bath (30 min., r.t.), and neutralized with 1 mol L−1 HCl. Afterwards, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter to separate large particles. For separation of the final fraction of CA-based CDs ultrafiltration devices were used with MWCO 50 kDa (Vivaspin 20 centrifugal concentrator, Sartorius). Thus, the solution of CA-based CDs was poured into a centrifugal concentrator and distilled water was added to 15 mL, then the ultrafiltration unit was centrifuged (10 min., 5000 min−1). Then, the filtrate was analyzed using a spectrofluorimeter, while the retentate was diluted with distilled water (15 mL) and centrifuged again using the same unit. Several runs of ultrafiltration were performed until no fluorescence was detected in the filtrate (see reference article for details [21]). Additionally, the final retentate was analyzed using LC-FLD to exclude the presence of low molecular fluorophores. Finally, the retentate was freeze-dried, and obtained CA-based CDs were stored in the freezer until further experiments. The obtained CA-based CDs were characterized using methods typically employed for this purpose including XPS, TEM, XRD, and optical properties characterization (UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra) (Table SX1 and Figures S7 and SX1-SX5) (For details see supplementary information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of real CDs samples: cys-CDs, ur-CDs-blue, ur-CDs-green

ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (0.97 g) and cysteine (1.82 g) (molar ratio 1:3) were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and placed in a reaction vial together with a magnetic stirrer. The reaction was carried out in a Q-Tube reaction apparatus, at 200 °C, under increased pressure for 3 h.

Citric acid (0.97 g) and urea (0.78 g) (molar ratio 1:3) were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and placed in a reaction vial together with a magnetic stirrer. The reaction was carried out in a Q-Tube reaction apparatus, at 200 °C, under increased pressure for 3 h.

Citric acid (0.77 g) and urea (0.78 g) (molar ratio 1:3) were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and placed in a reaction vial together with a magnetic stirrer. The reaction was performed in an open vessel under normal pressure at 200 °C for 3 h.

After synthesis, the reaction mixtures were all dissolved in distilled water using an ultrasonic bath, filtered through a funnel with sintered disc G4, and diluted so that the volume of each mixture was 100 mL. Subsequently, each mixture was partitioned into 4 equal parts (25 mL each), one was freeze-dried, two were subjected to dialysis (24 h and 72 h) and one was left frozen as a backup. Dialysis towards H2O was conducted with a MWCO 10 kDa membrane for 24 h and 72 h. Water (800 mL) was changed every 12 h during the first 24 h and then every 24 h in the case of the 72 h purification variant. After dialysis, CDs solutions from membranes were brought to a known volume, then the samples were taken for LC-FLD and LC-MS analysis (100 µL of each sample was taken and diluted to 1500 µL with distilled water). Afterwards, all CDs batches were frozen and freeze-dried. Subsequently, approximately 2 mg of samples were taken from each batch here: raw CDs, CDs after 24 h dialysis, and CDs after 72 h dialysis, dissolved in 0.7 mL D2O (using an ultrasonic bath) and 1H NMR analyses were performed.
Citrazinic acid was purchased from Merck (Germany), while TPA and HPPT were prepared according to protocols elucidated in our lab and reported earlier with modifications [14,37].

Anhydrous CA (1.92 g) (Alfa Aesar, USA) and urea (1.8 g) (Merck, Germany) were placed in a glass vial (15 mL) and heated using an aluminum heating block (140 °C, 120 min.). The reaction mixture was dissolved in deionized water and the target fraction (HPPT) was separated using preparative HPLC and freeze-dried (Alpha 2–4 LO Plus, 0.37 mbar, 3 days). A preparative HPLC system with LC-20AP pumps, UV–Vis SPD-20AV detector, and LabSolutions 5.51 operating software were used (Shimadzu Corp., Japan) equipped with preparative column C18 (250 mm × 50 mm i.d., 30 μm) (Interchim, France) with a 30 mm x 10 mm i.d. guard column of the same material under the following gradient system: (t[min], % A, % B, % C), (0, 100, 0, 0), (10, 100, 0, 0), (50, 0, 10, 90), (60, 0, 70, 30), (62, 0, 70, 30). The mobile phases were: A – demineralized water, B – pure acetone, C – 0.1 % formic acid in water. The injection volume was 30 mL and the flow rate was 50 mL min−1. Detection was performed at 404 and 254 nm with a PDA UV –Vis detector; column temp. 30 °C. The purity of the product was confirmed using LC-DAD-MS, while the chemical structure was confirmed via 1H and 13C NMR experiments (see chromatographic analyses section and supplementary information for details, Figures SX6-SX10).

Anhydrous CA (1.92 g) (Alfa Aesar, USA) and L-cysteine (1.21 g) (Fluorochem, United Kindom) were placed in a glass vial (15 mL) and heated using an aluminum heating block (140 °C, 120 min). The reaction mixture was dissolved in deionized water and the target fraction (TPA) was separated using preparative HPLC and freeze-dried (Alpha 2–4 LO Plus, 0.37 mbar, 3 days). A preparative HPLC system with LC-20AP pumps, UV–Vis SPD-20AV detector and LabSolutions 5.51 operating software were used (Shimadzu Corp., Japan) equipped with preparative column C18 (250 mm × 50 mm i.d., 30 μm) (Interchim, France) with a 30 mm x 10 mm i.d. guard column of the same material under the following gradient system: (t[min], % A, % B, % C), (0, 100, 0, 0), (10, 100, 0, 0), (50, 0, 10, 90), (60, 0, 70, 30), (62, 0, 70, 30). The mobile phases were: A – demineralized water, B – pure acetone, C – 0.1 % formic acid in water. The injection volume was 30 mL and the flow rate was 50 mL min−1. Detection was performed at 350 and 254 nm with a PDA UV –Vis detector; column temp. 30 °C. The purity of the product was confirmed using LC-DAD-MS, while the chemical structure was confirmed via 1H and 13C NMR experiments (see chromatographic analyses section and supplementary information for details, Figures SX11-SX15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of working solutions of the fluorophores and CA-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For each tested fluorophore two series of solutions were prepared i.e. with CA-based CDs and without CA-based CDs. Firstly, stock solutions of TPA and HPPT were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of a particular substance in 50 mL of distilled water. Then, working solutions of TPA and HPPT were prepared from the stock solutions by dilution with distilled water or with 0.21 mg mL−1 CA-based CDs aqueous solution to reach concentrations listed in Table S2. Due to the lower solubility of CZA (∼0.16 mg mL−1), its solutions were prepared separately. Firstly, a stock solution of CZA in distilled water was prepared (0.147 mg mL−1), by dissolving 14.7 mg CZA in 100 mL of water. Then, a stock solution of CZA+CA-based CDs was prepared by dissolving 14.7 mg CZA in 100 mL of 0.16 mg mL−1 CA-based CDs aqueous solution. Finally, the stock solutions were diluted either with water or with 0.16 mg mL−1 CA-based CDs to reach concentrations listed in Table S1.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of studied solutions (Table S2) were acquired using UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the 280–500 nm range. Quartz cuvettes with a 10 mm optical path were used. The calibration curves were plotted using absorption values measured at λmax in the long wavelength region (>320 nm).

Fluorescence emission spectra of studied solutions (Table S2) were acquired using RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). PL emission and excitation slits were set at 5 nm. For solutions containing different fluorophores, diverse excitation wavelengths (λex) were used (CZA λex = 347 nm, TPA λex = 347 nm, HPPT λex = 405 nm). The calibration curves were plotted using PL emission intensity values measured at λmax.

For analytical low-resolution LC-ESI-MS analyses LCMS-8030 (Shimadzu, Japan) mass spectrometric system coupled to an LC-20ADXR pump utilizing the LC gradient was used. The LC analyses were carried out on a 100 mm × 4.6 mm × 5.0 μm Kinetex C18 chromatographic column (Phenomenex, USA). The Kinetex C18 chromatographic column was preceded by a guard column of the same material (Phenomenex, USA). The positive and negative ion chromatograms as well as mass spectra were acquired (electrospray voltage 4.5 kV; capillary temperature 250 °C), and controlled via LabSolutions software version 5.91 (Shimadzu, Japan). For detection and quantification Single Ion Monitoring (SIM) modes (positive and negative ionization) were used. Both negative (154, 179, 240 m/z) and positive (156, 181, 242 m/z) ions were monitored. Above m/z values reflect the pseudomolecular ions of the studied fluorophores; CZA (155 g mol−1), HPPT (180 g mol−1), and TPA (241 g mol−1). Calibration curves were plotted using peak area obtained from particular SIM chromatograms. The MS unit was preceded by DAD (SPD-M20A, Shimadzu) and FLD (RF-20Axs, Shimadzu) detectors in the analytical system (both controlled via LabSolutions software version 5.91). The DAD detector acquired absorption spectra in 190–800 nm range. For FLD detector, samples were excited at 345 nm and the PL emission chromatograms were collected at 425 nm (CZA and TPA) or λex = 405 nm and λem = 530 nm (HPPT). The calibration curves were obtained using peak area of specific peaks in FLD chromatograms. For DAD detector, chromatograms at specific absorption wavelength were extracted first, and then the calibration curves were obtained using specific peak area. The injection volume was 2 μL, and the flow rate was 1 mL min−1. The column was thermostated at 40 °C. The separation of the analytes was performed with binary gradient elution. The mobile phases were: A – 2 % formic acid in the demineralized water, and B – pure methanol. The gradient profile was: (t [min], % B), (0, 0.1), (7, 0.1), (13, 30), (13.01, 90), (14, 90).

1H NMR spectra were acquired at 14.1 T a Bruker AVANCE III 600 spectrometer operating at 600.27 with D2O as a solvent. 1H NMR spectra were recorded with 18 μs 90° pulse width, relaxation delay 10 s, acquisition time 2.73 s, 128 scans. All samples (Table S6) were measured at 295 K under the same experimental parameters. The chemical shifts were calibrated using water signal δ = 4.80 ppm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of real CDs samples at different purification stages
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs samples at various stages of purification were subjected to 1H NMR analyses with a D2O as a solvent. The analyses were conducted on a JEOL 500 MHz FT-NMR instrument (JNM-ECZR500 RS1 version ECZR), with a magnet of 11.74 T field. 1HNMR spectra were recorded with 3.60 μs 90° pulse width, relaxation delay 4 s, acquisition time 2.05 s, 8 scans. The chemical shifts were calibrated using water signal δ = 4.80 ppm. All samples were measured at 295 K under the same experimental parameters. The spectra and interpreted results of the analyses are provided in the Supporting Information.

Alicja Wysocka: Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Łukasz Waluda: Formal analysis, Investigation. Rafał Konefał: Formal analysis, Investigation. Wiktor Kasprzyk: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Wiktor Kasprzyk reports financial support and equipment, drugs, or supplies were provided by National Centre for Research and Development. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from the Polish National Center for Research and Development, Lider XII (grant number: LIDER/53/0277/L-12/20/NCBR/2021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111240.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
